purpose: Maps the two-dimensional parameter space (core inflation × fiscal deficit/GDP) that determines whether stock-bond correlation is stable (resolved to positive or negative) or structurally unstable (oscillating).
components: X-axis: Core inflation rate with thresholds at 2.0% (lower) and 3.5% (upper)
Y-axis: Fiscal deficit as percent of GDP with thresholds at 4.5% (lower) and 7.0% (upper)
Below corridor on inflation (<2.0%) OR deficit (<4.5% declining): negative correlation dominates
Above corridor on inflation (>3.5-4.0% sustained): positive correlation locks in
Inside corridor: structurally unstable, oscillating correlation with maturity bifurcation
Current US position: core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP — interior of corridor
Core inflation dimension: <2.0% → negative correlation stable; 2.0-3.5% → unstable; >4.0% → positive correlation locks in
Fiscal deficit dimension: <4.0% declining → negative correlation; 4.5-7.0% → unstable; potentially higher → positive locks in
Current US position: core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP (inside corridor)
Exit conditions: movement outside corridor on either dimension resolves regime
current_assessment: More operationally useful than unidimensional inflation focus. The US clearly occupies the corridor. Parameter boundaries carry ±1pp uncertainty. The concept is more robust than the specific numerical boundaries. Derived from 4 G10 episodes.
limitations: Derived from only 4 G10 episodes — small sample for parameter calibration
Does not account for structural differences across economies (currency issuer vs. user, reserve currency status)
The concept is more robust than the specific numerical boundaries
Does not specify transition dynamics when exiting the corridor
Parameter boundaries derive from small sample (4 G10 episodes)
±1pp boundary uncertainty is significant relative to corridor width
Does not specify dynamics within the corridor (path dependency)
May not account for structural breaks (AI productivity, passive investing)
